mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar XF 2.7d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned XF 2.7d (276DT) engine typically costs £2,500–£4,500 supply only. Fitting adds £1,500–£2,500 depending on location and workshop rates. A remanufactured Jaguar XF diesel engine is available from specialist suppliers at approximately £3,068. Main dealer new engines start from £6,000–£8,000+ supply only. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Jaguar XF 2.7d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 2.7d has a mixed reputation. The 2.7 is 'just an early version of the 3.0V6' with known issues including snapped crankshafts, split plastic intakes and spun bearings. However, 'most, if properly maintained are just great'. Properly maintained, the 2.7 is 'a reliable unit'. Check the Era Map above for your specific engine.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Jaguar XF 2.7d?

acceptedAnswer.text: The main known issues are: (1) Crankshaft and bearing failure — the most devastating fault, (2) Fuel injector failure — the Siemens piezo injector system is troublesome, (3) DPF clogging — the engine 'doesn't like short runs', and (4) Oil leaks — especially through the heat exchanger.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Jaguar XF 2.7d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept £4,000–£10,000 XF, a £3,000–£4,500 recon is 30–110% of the car's value. For a clean example with good body and no other issues, replacement may be worthwhile. However, if the car has other issues (air suspension, electrical faults), evaluate total repair costs before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Jaguar XF 2.7d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 2.7d uses the 276DT engine code (also known as AJD). It's a 2.7-litre V6 diesel producing 207 hp (152 kW) and 435 Nm of torque. The engine code is stamped on the cylinder block. Tell us your registration and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the 2.7d crankshaft issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: If caught early, bearing replacement may be possible (£1,500–£3,000). However, if the crankshaft has snapped or bearings have spun, a full engine replacement is usually the only reliable fix (£4,000–£7,000). At the first sign of rhythmic knocking or an oil pressure warning, stop driving and arrange recovery to a specialist immediately.

eraMap.title: Jaguar XF 2.7d — Which Engine Does Your Car Actually Have?

rows.eraNote: 2.7L V6 diesel — 207 hp, 435 Nm. Known for crankshaft failure, fuel injector and DPF issues

replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an XF 2.7d engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer).

cta.label: → Compare XF 2.7d engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Crankshaft & Bearing Failure (Catastrophic)
problems.symptoms: Rhythmic knocking from lower engine block that worsens under load, oil pressure warning, sudden engine seizure with no warning
problems.repairCost: £1,500–£3,000 (early intervention — bearing replacement if caught immediately)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: stop driving immediately if you hear knocking or see the oil pressure warning

problems.recommendation: This is the most devastating fault affecting the 2.7d engine. Known issues include "snapped crankshafts" and "spun bearings". One owner reported a "2008 XF 2.7 diesel with crank that is knocking and appears to have snapped". The 3.0-litre engine was updated to add a way of securing the bearings for the crankshaft. At the first sign of rhythmic knocking or an oil pressure warning, stop driving and arrange recovery to a specialist. A £1,500–£3,000 early intervention is dramatically cheaper than a £4,000–£7,000 engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full crankshaft failure guide → /failures/ajd-v6-crankshaft-bearing-failure/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

problems.title: Fuel Injector Failure (Siemens System)
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, misfires, increased fuel consumption, black smoke, hard starting, engine warning light
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not progress to engine replacement if addressed)
problems.recommendation: The most troublesome component on the 2.7d engine is the Siemens fuel system with piezo injectors. These injectors are prone to failure and can cause rough running, misfires, and poor fuel economy. A new injector costs approximately £327. If you experience rough running or increased fuel consumption, have the injectors tested. Catching injector wear early prevents more serious engine damage.

failureLink.label: Read the full injector failure guide

problems.title: DPF Clogging (Short Urban Journeys)
problems.symptoms: DPF warning light, reduced performance, increased fuel consumption, limp mode, "Engine Systems Fault" warning
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — short urban journeys accelerate the issue
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not progress to engine replacement if addressed)
problems.recommendation: The 2.7d engine "doesn't like short runs due to the DPF system". DPF clogging is a common issue on vehicles used primarily for short urban journeys. One owner reported replacing the DPF for approximately £250. Regular motorway runs are essential to allow passive DPF regeneration. If the warning appears, book a forced regeneration or have the DPF cleaned before it becomes clogged beyond repair.

failureLink.label: Read the full DPF clogging guide

problems.title: Oil Leaks (Heat Exchanger & Seals)
problems.symptoms: Burning oil smell, oil spots under the car, visible oil residue around the engine, low oil level warning
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not progress to engine replacement if addressed)

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection if you notice oil spots or burning smell

problems.recommendation: The 2.7d engine suffers from "massive problem" oil leaks, especially through the heat exchanger. Other weak points include the thermostat, EGR valve and crankshaft oil seals. If you notice a burning smell or oil spots, have the gaskets inspected promptly. Catching leaks early prevents oil contamination of other components and more serious engine damage.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil leak guide → /failures/ajd-v6-oil-leaks/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

buyingChecks.title: Buying This XF 2.7d? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with regular oil changes using correct 5W-30 oil
* Evidence of regular motorway driving (DPF health)
* No history of knocking or oil pressure warnings
avoidIf: * Any history of knocking or oil pressure warnings — this is a critical warning sign
* Diesel smell on dipstick or rising oil level (injector seal failure)
* DPF warning lights or "Engine Systems Fault" messages
* Poor service history — the 2.7d is unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The XF 2.7d's 276DT engine offers a compelling blend of performance and economy — 207 hp and 435 Nm in a sleek executive saloon. However, it has well-documented weaknesses: crankshaft and bearing failure is the most devastating issue, followed by injector problems, DPF clogging, and oil leaks. The 2.7 is "just an early version of the 3.0V6", and the 3.0 was updated to address bearing security. Buy with full service history and evidence of regular long-distance driving. Without history? Proceed with caution — the repair bills can be substantial.

cta.label: → Compare XF 2.7d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Jaguar XF 2.7d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for knocking or rattling that worsens with revs (crankshaft warning)
* Oil Pressure Warning — Has it ever flickered? This is a critical warning sign
* Dipstick Check — Smell for diesel (injector seal failure); check for rising oil level
* Service History — Including the 276DT engine and confirmed 10,000-mile oil change intervals — non-negotiable
* DPF Health — Ask about driving patterns; short urban journeys are a red flag
* Injector Health — Any rough idle, misfires, or black smoke?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Look for injector or DPF-related codes
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
